summary: Gruber writes a heartfelt tribute to David Foster Wallace following his suicide, reflecting on what made Wallace's writing uniquely brilliant. He describes Wallace's prose as possessing 'watchmaker-like precision' and notes that Wallace was still improving, his later work having shed any tendency toward showmanship in favor of compassion and truth. Gruber expresses the magnitude of the loss — not just of what Wallace wrote, but of the words yet to come. The second half offers reading recommendations for those new to Wallace's work, suggesting his non-fiction essays as the most approachable entry point.
key_points: Wallace is the only writer whose work made Gruber ache with the desire to write like that — a singular admiration among dozens of writers he respects
Wallace's trajectory was still ascending: his later work had matured past linguistic showmanship into something more compassionate and true
The tragedy is compounded by the loss of future work that will never exist
Gruber recommends Wallace's non-fiction essays as the best entry point, with 'Everything and More' specifically for the mathematically inclined
money_quotes: David Foster Wallace possessed a verbal gas pedal that he could press further than what I had ever thought possible.
Wallace is the only one whose work consistently makes me ache with the impossible desire: I wish I could write like that.
The loss is too big, the circumstances too senseless.

The occasional tendency toward linguistic showmanship for the sake of showmanship was gone. What was left was an exuberant dedication that left no heavy thought unconsidered, no detail unexamined, no apt digression unexplored.
A palpable authorial compassion for the reader. What was left was the truth.
What we've lost, the words that were yet to come, we'll never know.
No one else but David Foster Wallace could have written this.
key_insight: Wallace's death is an outsized tragedy because he was still ascending — his later work had refined raw verbal brilliance into something rarer: precision married to compassion.
